topic: A student learns their thesis advisor is leaving the university
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a physical blow. Subject line: Departure Notice. My stomach clenched, a tight, cold knot forming beneath my ribs. Professor Albright was leaving. It wasn't a casual announcement; it was final. I stared at the screen, the fluorescent lights of the library suddenly buzzing with an aggressive intensity. Every sound—the rustle of paper, the distant hum of the ventilation—seemed amplified, pressing in on me. My hands started to tremble, the edges of the keyboard blurring slightly. I quickly minimized the message, but the image of his empty office, the space where his meticulously organized notes used to reside, remained burned behind my eyelids. I needed to breathe, but the air felt thin, insufficient.

[story 2]

"So, you're actually leaving?" I managed, the words sounding brittle even to my own ears. My voice cracked on the final syllable. Dr. Hemlock looked up from his desk, a slow, deliberate movement that made my breath hitch in my throat. He didn't offer a reassuring smile; his expression was flat, almost distant. I felt a sudden, overwhelming urge to retreat, to physically shrink into the corner of the study carrel. My palms were slick against the worn textbook cover. *This is it,* a frantic voice whispered in my head. The weight of the next few months, the unknown landscape of my thesis without his guidance, settled onto my shoulders like a heavy stone. I just wanted the floor to swallow me whole.

[story 3]

I kept glancing at the clock on the wall, the seconds ticking with agonizing slowness. The official notification had been posted on the departmental bulletin board, stark black text against the beige laminate. My heart hammered against my ribs, a frantic drumbeat I was certain everyone in the vicinity could hear. I stood up abruptly, knocking my chair slightly askew. My legs felt unsteady, like they were submerged in water. I walked to the window, pulling the curtain aside just a fraction. Outside, the campus seemed too bright, too loud. Every shadow seemed to deepen, taking on menacing shapes. I gripped the windowsill, my knuckles turning white, desperately trying to anchor myself against the swirling uncertainty that was beginning to take hold.
